text: What is the result of the following program?
explanation: Java is pass-by-value. The method modifies its local copy of x, not the original variable a. So a remains 15.
source: Selikoff & Boyarsky, OCP Java SE 21 Developer Study Guide (Sybex, 2023), Chapter 5 – Methods, pp. 214–215

text: What is the output of the following program?

options.text: The code compiles but throws an exception at runtime.

explanation: Fields (not methods) are not polymorphic — they are accessed based on the reference type. i is declared as Insect, so i.numberOfLegs gives Insect's 4. After casting to Beetle, the Beetle field gives 6.
source: Selikoff & Boyarsky, OCP Java SE 21 Developer Study Guide (Sybex, 2023), Chapter 8 – Class Design, pp. 379–381

text: Which of the following can be used to implement a functional interface? (Choose all that apply.)

options.text: An anonymous class with a single abstract method
A concrete class implementing the interface

explanation: Functional interfaces can be implemented by anonymous classes, lambdas, method references, or regular classes. var cannot implement an interface on its own.
source: Selikoff & Boyarsky, OCP Java SE 21 Developer Study Guide (Sybex, 2023), Chapter 7 – Beyond Classes, pp. 316–319

text: Which of the following are valid subclasses of a sealed class? (Choose all that apply.)
code: public sealed class Shape permits Circle, Rectangle, Triangle {}

options.text: public final class Circle extends Shape {}
public sealed class Rectangle extends Shape permits Square {}
public non-sealed class Triangle extends Shape {}
public abstract class Triangle extends Shape {}

explanation: Direct subclasses of a sealed class must be declared final, sealed, or non-sealed. Square is not permitted, so D is invalid. Abstract classes without sealed/non-sealed are invalid (E).
source: Selikoff & Boyarsky, OCP Java SE 21 Developer Study Guide (Sybex, 2023), Chapter 7 – Beyond Classes, pp. 344–348

text: What is the output of the following code?

options.text: Compilation error: records cannot have custom constructors.

explanation: Records support compact constructors (without parameter list) for validation. The compact constructor runs first, then the record assigns the fields. p.x() returns 3, p.y() returns 4. No exception since x >= 0.
source: Selikoff & Boyarsky, OCP Java SE 21 Developer Study Guide (Sybex, 2023), Chapter 7 – Beyond Classes, pp. 348–354

text: Which of the following are true about records in Java 21? (Choose all that apply.)

options.text: The compiler generates equals(), hashCode(), and toString() automatically.
Record components are mutable by default.

explanation: Records are implicitly final and cannot extend any class (they implicitly extend java.lang.Record). They can implement interfaces. The compiler auto-generates equals(), hashCode(), toString(), and accessor methods. Record components are final (immutable).
source: Selikoff & Boyarsky, OCP Java SE 21 Developer Study Guide (Sybex, 2023), Chapter 7 – Beyond Classes, pp. 348–354

text: What is the output of the following code?
explanation: When a class implements two interfaces with conflicting default methods, it must override the method. The override uses InterfaceName.super.method() to call a specific interface's default. Greet.super.hello() returns "Hello".
source: Selikoff & Boyarsky, OCP Java SE 21 Developer Study Guide (Sybex, 2023), Chapter 7 – Beyond Classes, pp. 305–310

text: What is the output of the following code?

options.text: Compilation error: abstract class cannot have a constructor.

explanation: Abstract classes can and often do have constructors, called via super() from subclasses. describe() is a concrete method that calls the abstract sound() — which is polymorphically resolved to Dog.sound() at runtime. Output: 'Rex says woof'.
source: Selikoff & Boyarsky, OCP Java SE 21 Developer Study Guide (Sybex, 2023), Chapter 8 – Class Design, pp. 363–370

text: Which of the following correctly demonstrates method overloading vs overriding?
explanation: (1) has the same method signature (same name and parameter type int) — it overrides the parent method. (2) has a different parameter type (double) — it is a new overloaded method in the child class.
source: Selikoff & Boyarsky, OCP Java SE 21 Developer Study Guide (Sybex, 2023), Chapter 8 – Class Design, pp. 371–380

text: Which of the following are rules for method overriding in Java? (Choose all that apply.)

options.text: The overriding method must have the same method signature.
The overriding method can declare fewer checked exceptions than the overridden method.
The overriding method can have a wider access modifier.
The overriding method can return a subtype of the original return type (covariant return).

explanation: Overriding requires the same signature. Checked exceptions can be the same or a subset (not broader). Access modifier can be the same or wider (public > protected > package-private). Covariant return types are allowed. Static methods are hidden, not overridden.
source: Selikoff & Boyarsky, OCP Java SE 21 Developer Study Guide (Sybex, 2023), Chapter 8 – Class Design, pp. 371–380

text: What is the output of the following code?
explanation: When Derived() calls super(), Base() runs and calls print(). Due to polymorphism, Derived.print() is called. However, at this point, the Derived instance is not fully initialized — value has not been assigned (it is still 0). So 'Derived: 0' is printed.
source: Selikoff & Boyarsky, OCP Java SE 21 Developer Study Guide (Sybex, 2023), Chapter 8 – Class Design, pp. 355–363

text: Which of the following about enums in Java are true?

options.text: Enum constants can have their own method implementations.
Both B and C are true.

explanation: Enums implicitly extend java.lang.Enum so they cannot extend other classes. They CAN implement interfaces. Each enum constant can also override methods — this is the constant-specific class body pattern.
source: Selikoff & Boyarsky, OCP Java SE 21 Developer Study Guide (Sybex, 2023), Chapter 7 – Beyond Classes, pp. 335–344

text: What is the output of the following inner class code?
explanation: (1) x refers to the local variable = 30. (2) this.x refers to Inner's field = 20. (3) Outer.this.x refers to the enclosing Outer instance's field = 10.
source: Selikoff & Boyarsky, OCP Java SE 21 Developer Study Guide (Sybex, 2023), Chapter 7 – Beyond Classes, pp. 319–327

text: Which of the following are true about interfaces in Java 21? (Choose all that apply.)

options.text: Interfaces can have default methods with a body.
All interface fields are implicitly public static final.

explanation: Since Java 9, interfaces can have private methods (helper for default/static methods) and static methods. Since Java 8, interfaces can have default methods. Interface fields are always public static final. Interfaces cannot have instance (non-static) fields.
source: Selikoff & Boyarsky, OCP Java SE 21 Developer Study Guide (Sybex, 2023), Chapter 7 – Beyond Classes, pp. 293–315

text: What is the output of the following code using a static nested class?

options.text: Compilation error: static nested class cannot access outer fields.
Compilation error: new Nested() requires an outer instance.

explanation: A static nested class can access static members of the enclosing class. It does NOT require an instance of the outer class to be instantiated (unlike inner classes). new Nested() is valid in a static context.
source: Selikoff & Boyarsky, OCP Java SE 21 Developer Study Guide (Sybex, 2023), Chapter 7 – Beyond Classes, pp. 327–332
